themes.name: Dust, Glory & Forgotten Chapters
themes.tagline: From MiG Alley to Ithaca — the stories history textbooks were too busy to finish.

items.title: The Forgotten Air Campaign: Korea, 1950
items.channel_title: The History Guy: History Deserves to Be Remembered
items.description:
The air war in Korea is often remembered for the first jet on jet combats and the dreaded “MiG alley.”  But a far less remembered part of the air war, before a MiG entered the fray, represented a key, if almost completely overlooked, moment in one of the hottest times of the Cold War. 

Check out our new shop for fun The History Guy merchandise:
https://thehistoryguy-shop.fourthwall.com/products/thg-history

This is original content based on research by The History Guy. Images in the Public Doma

items.title: Why Odysseus Is The Worst Hero
items.description:
What if the world’s most famous homecoming was really a nightmare of monsters, gods, and one very stubborn king trying to get back to Ithaca?

In our new series Tristan Hughes and classicist Dr. Emily Hauser dive into Homer’s Odyssey, an epic of shipwrecks, sorcery, giants and divine meddling, where Odysseus is never quite sure whether the next shore will save him or destroy him.

They trace his journey from the ruins of Troy to Calypso’s island, the Phaeacians, Circe, the Cyclops, and even the

items.title: Henry VIII's Tennis Court at Hampton Court Palace
items.description:
Did you know that Henry VIII's palace had a tennis court? Tennis, or to be precise, lawn tennis, is derived from an ancient game. Once enjoyed by the masses, it later became the preserve of the wealthy. We call this sport Real Tennis to distinguish it from the modern game. And it was this that was incredibly popular at the Tudor court.

In this video, HRP Curator Charles Farris talks to Real Tennis professional and historian Leslie Ronaldson at the Royal Tennis Court at Hampton Court Palace to l

items.title: New York - 1600s
items.description:
Step into 17th-century New York with an AI reconstruction inspired by colonial maps, traveler accounts, and early Dutch and English city plans. Using records from the Dutch West India Company, English surveyors, and contemporary sketches, this visualisation interprets the settlement’s shift from New Amsterdam to New York while staying true to its materials and street patterns.

During this century, the town changed quickly: timber houses lined Pearl Street, Fort Amsterdam guarded the harbor, and

themes.name: The Curious Physics of Desire
themes.tagline: Love, gemstones, and fast food empires — things we want, why we want them, and what they're really worth.

items.title: Scientists Finally Figured Out What Love Is For
items.description:
The dumb things we do for love make a lot more sense than you think…
↓↓↓ More info and sources below ↓↓↓

Love makes people do ridiculous things: quit jobs, cross continents, build monuments, and ignore every red flag in sight. But what if that's exactly what love evolved to do? This video explores the neuroscience, psychology, and evolutionary biology of romance to explain why love can feel irrational—and why that may have helped make us human.

References: https://sites.google.com/view/purpose

items.title: Every Gemstone Ranked (Some Are Overpriced)
items.description:
Gemstones are sold through rarity, prestige, and centuries of mythology, but none of that changes the physics. A stone can be rare, expensive, and famous, and still be a terrible choice for a ring.

In this video I rank some of the most common gemstones from best to worst based on durability, aesthetics, and value for money.

I’m Mason, a jeweller and founder of Urban Sterling. On this channel I explore the materials science and craft behind jewellery. The industry is extremely opaque, and much

items.title: The Inevitable Downfall Of Jack In The Box
items.description:
While McDonald’s, Burger King, and Wendy’s dominate the fast-food burger world, Jack in the Box has long been carving out a surprising legacy of its own. From its creepy clown mascot to the iconic Jumbo Jack, Jack in the Box seemingly has everything it needs to compete at the top... but lately, things haven't been going so well for Jack. 

In this episode of Weird History, we dive into the strange rise and fall of one of America’s most unique and unforgettable fast food chains.

#jackinthebox #f

themes.name: Roots, Soil & Slow Living
themes.tagline: Growing your own food, restoring a château, and island-hopping the Azores — a love letter to putting down roots.

items.title: They Bought an Abandoned Plot and Grew THIS (80% Self-Sufficient)
items.description:
For more about Dan and Laurie's garden adventures, you can follow their YouTube channel here: @freedomforestlife 

How much land would it take to feed ourselves almost entirely to the point of being virtually self-sufficient? And what would that look like? Which plants would we need to grow and just HOW HEALTHY would we look if we ate 80% of our food directly from the land we grow it on? Well, Dan and Laurie at Freedom Forest Life can answer all these questions in this jaw-dropping garden tour o

morning_soundtrack.snippet: Campbell co-wrote a number of hits for Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers. His new band is The Dirty Knobs, and their album is Mission of Mercy. Low Cut Connie's new album is Livin in the USA.

Brunner also makes his own music under the name Strawberry Hands. Today, he announces plans to follow his 2020 album I Advance

That news came packaged with her debut single "Gone Girl." Today, she's released a new single "Elvis Queen," which announces h

morning_soundtrack.snippet: EDITOR’S NOTE: No Depression’s new “Top 5” series features a quick playlist in which roots musicians share what they’re listening to at the moment. It gives artists the chance to highlight their contemporaries, as well as classic songs getting them through

His new release, Father Time & Mother Nature is at Americana and Texas radio

morning_soundtrack.snippet: On Bass Womb Room, several new compositions thread together an unvaulted set of ’90s demos

Released barely a year after Rumours, Mark & Suzann Farmer's 1978 private-press version trades California studio polish

morning_soundtrack.title: The Spirit of Redbone’s Ghost Dance at Wounded Knee
morning_soundtrack.snippet: "We Were All Wounded at Wounded Knee" was omitted from Redbone's 1973 US-version album Wovoka, but it was top of the charts in parts of Europe.

morning_soundtrack.title: Fatoumata Diawara Creates Inspiring Pop on ‘Massa’
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Fatoumata Diawara is a charismatic performer whose skill set has extraordinary breadth and depth, and Massa is her fullest demonstration of it thus far.

morning_soundtrack.title: Alden Hellmuth Excels on Her Astonishing New Album
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Alden Hellmuth is both blazing trails and paying tribute to the past, resulting in what may very well be the finest jazz album of the year so far.

global_silver_linings.title: Biotech Startup Breeds Blight Tolerant American Chestnut Without Any Genetic Defects
global_silver_linings.snippet: At the intersection of big data, forestry, and genomics, a biotech startup has birthed a generation of “elite” blight-resistant American chestnut trees without deep genetic tinkering. The company is inspired by saving American hardwoods that have been ravaged by an introduced fungal disease, and says their proprietary approach could be replicated to help save American […] The post Biotech Startup Breeds Blight Tolerant American Chestnut Without Any Genetic Defects appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: The American chestnut, once a cornerstone of Eastern forests, was nearly wiped out by blight a century ago — and now scientists have bred a healthy, blight-tolerant tree that could restore this beloved species to the wild. It's a quiet triumph for both conservation and the patient, determined people who never stopped believing the forest could heal.

global_silver_linings.title: The underground fridge that keeps food cool – even in a heatwave
global_silver_linings.snippet: Inspired by olden days ice houses, this underground fridge uses the earth’s stable temperature to keep food cool The post The underground fridge that keeps food cool – even in a heatwave appeared first on Positive News .
global_silver_linings.reason: A simple, low-tech underground cooling system is helping communities keep food fresh without electricity — proving that ancient wisdom and clever design can offer real solutions to modern climate challenges. It's a hopeful reminder that not every answer to a big problem has to be complicated or expensive.

global_silver_linings.title: What If We Never Built Another New Building?
global_silver_linings.snippet: Swiss architect Barbara Buser has spent decades proving that the greenest building is often the one already standing. When one unscrews a section of the bright red sheet metal façade from K.118, a former carpentry factory in Winterthur, Switzerland, the interior reveals something few people expect to find inside an... The post What If We Never Built Another New Building?
global_silver_linings.reason: A growing movement of architects and planners is making the case for retrofitting and reimagining existing buildings rather than constantly building new ones, slashing the carbon footprint of construction in the process. It turns out that caring for what we already have — in housing, as in nature — might be one of the most radical and hopeful acts we can take.

discovery_articles.title: Saihō-ji in Kyoto, Japan
discovery_articles.snippet: The city of Kyoto has over 1,600 Buddhist temples. While this is a very large number, it often feels like this isn’t enough. Famous sites such as Kinkaku-ji , Kiyomizu-dera , and Ryōan-ji are often seen over the shoulders of hundreds of other visitors, who tend to be cropped out of social media posts.

discovery_articles.title: The Limberlost Cabin in Geneva, Indiana
discovery_articles.snippet: The Limberlost Cabin was the home of famed Indiana author Gene Stratton-Porter, who designed and lived in the home in Geneva, Indiana, from 1895 to 1913. Mrs. Stratton-Porter was born in nearby Wabash County, Indiana, in 1863.

discovery_articles.title: Corrimony Chambered Cairn in Corrimony, Scotland
discovery_articles.snippet: Corrimony Chambered Cairn, nestled in the Glen Urquhart area of the Scottish Highlands, is a striking example of a Bronze Age burial site, thought to date back more than 4,000 years. This cairn is part of the Clava type, a style commonly associated with the region, and it offers a glimpse into ancient funerary practices and beliefs. The cairn features a circular stone structure about 15 meters in diameter, with a passage leading to a central burial chamber.

discovery_articles.title: Elmhurst Sculpture Garden in Queens, New York
discovery_articles.snippet: The strips of green next to the highway offramps tend to be pretty desolate, populated with weeds and trash. The pocket next to where the Long Island Expressway lets drivers off onto Queens Boulevard was no exception, until a group of sculptors decided to carve out a space for local art. The space was already being used sporadically by people in the neighborhood, but it was local artist Susan Vario who asked another artist, Yvonne Shortt, to collaborate on a project to turn it into a true community space.

discovery_articles.title: Did These Ancient Egyptian Princesses Wield Weapons and Train in Martial Arts, Just Like the Men of Their Time?
discovery_articles.snippet: A new analysis of their bones suggests they were buried alongside arrows, maces and daggers because they had repeatedly used those weapons in life

discovery_articles.title: Koalas Are at Risk of Extinction in Some Parts of Australia. Can Freezing Their Sperm and Eggs Help Save the Iconic Marsupials?
discovery_articles.snippet: Scientists are preserving the animals' reproductive cells in liquid nitrogen in a bid to boost genetic diversity in future populations

discovery_articles.title: Poop From Southern Right Whales Reveals That the Animals Might Be More Resilient to Climate Change Than We Thought
discovery_articles.snippet: Scientists long believed that these whales, which swim throughout the Southern Hemisphere, mainly feasted on krill and small crustaceans called copepods, which are disappearing from the warming ocean. A new study suggests that the cetaceans have a more varied, flexible diet

discovery_articles.title: In painted caves, ancient human DNA clings to the walls
discovery_articles.snippet: Scientists recovered ancient human DNA from cave walls for the first time. Refined, the method could help link prehistoric paintings to their makers.

discovery_articles.title: AI ‘ghosts’ can comfort mourners — even when the bots get the facts wrong
discovery_articles.snippet: A small study suggests emotional fit, not perfect accuracy, may make chatbot versions of dead loved ones feel real.

discovery_articles.title: The Skinner revolution
discovery_articles.snippet: A 1979 doc examining the life and ideas of B F Skinner, with a focus on his radical, controversial behaviour experiments - by Aeon Video Watch on Aeon

discovery_articles.title: Beyond Greece and Rome
discovery_articles.snippet: A dramatic shift in perspective is producing a much bigger, broader and more exciting view of ‘the ancient world’ - by Carlos Noreña Read on Aeon

discovery_articles.title: What Happened to the Bones of the American Bison?
discovery_articles.snippet: The near-extinction of the bison sparked an industry that left almost nothing of the animals behind. The post What Happened to the Bones of the American Bison? appeared first on JSTOR Daily .

discovery_articles.title: The Organisms That Make Earth’s Harshest Places Home
discovery_articles.snippet: Extremophiles that thrive in the most unforgiving environments aren’t just biological curiosities. Understanding their resilience has many implications for us. The post The Organisms That Make Earth’s Harshest Places Home first appeared on Quanta Magazine

discovery_articles.title: A High-Definition Cinematic Video Relives NASA’s Artemis II Mission
discovery_articles.snippet: John D. Boswell, a musician and filmmaker who works as MELODYSHEEP, created the film from videos and images published by NASA. Do stories and artists like this matter to you?

featured_read.title: From Mitochondria to Minds: The Illustrated Science of the Psychedelic Dream Called Life
featured_read.snippet: Like consciousness, like music, like love, life itself didn’t have to exist — nothing in the fundamental laws demands it. The universe could have gone on lapping at itself as a horizonless sea of pure spacetime, black and lifeless as the inside of a skull. But, in a whisper that soon became a symphony, life arose to give us such ceaseless wonders as the ghost pipe and the ginkgo, the bowerbird and the eye of the scallop.
featured_read.blurb: This illustrated meditation asks why life exists at all — not as a scientific problem to solve, but as a strangeness to sit inside. It moves from mitochondria to consciousness with the unhurried wonder of someone who genuinely can't believe any of this is happening.

garden_note.note: The long Zürich evening still holds warmth well past nine, and the garden exhales that particular scent of sun-baked earth and basil that makes high summer feel like a small, private luxury. Take a slow turn between the rows before the light goes — the beans are climbing with suspicious ambition, and the courgettes have almost certainly doubled since this morning.
in_season: Courgettes and summer squash at peak
Deadheading roses to encourage a late flush
garden_note.sky_tonight: The first-quarter Moon rides high in the south after dusk — half-lit and sharp-edged, it sets around midnight, leaving the later hours beautifully dark for Scorpius low on the southern horizon.
garden_note.sun_times: Dusk lingers until 21:52 tonight, with sunset at 21:16 — savour every last minute of it.

puzzle.label: The Evening Enigma
puzzle.prompt:
Two of these are true. One is a well-dressed lie. Which is it?

1. Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.
2. A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
3. The shortest war in recorded history lasted approximately six hours, fought between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896.
puzzle.answer: Number 3 is the lie: the Anglo-Zanzibar War lasted between 38 and 45 minutes, not six hours — making it even more remarkably brief than the false version suggests.
puzzle.hint: One of these involves a war so short it was over before most people finished breakfast.

previous_puzzle.label: The Morning Haiku
previous_puzzle.prompt:
open range

(Fill in the missing word.)

fern_data.greeting: Something about the end of a long summer day makes the forgotten feel worth retrieving — the air campaign nobody remembers from Korea, the science that finally named what love is actually doing, the American chestnut quietly coming back from the dead.
